perturbation.original_text: In that case, or at any time upon the written request of either disclosing party for any reason, each receiving party will promptly deliver to the disclosing party or destroy all Evaluation Material that was furnished to the receiving party or its Representatives by or on behalf of the disclosing party, by whichever method requested by the disclosing party, pursuant hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: In that case, or at any time upon the written request of either disclosing party for any reason, each receiving party will deliver to the disclosing party or destroy all Evaluation Material that was furnished to the receiving party or its Representatives by or on behalf of the disclosing party, by whichever method requested by the disclosing party, pursuant hereto, but no sooner than 90 days from the date of the request.
perturbation.explanation: The change introduces a delay of 90 days before the return or destruction of confidential material is required. This delay can be problematic as it doesn't align with the expectation of prompt action following a termination decision.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While New York law recognizes the duty of good faith in contracts, imposing a 90-day delay before returning confidential materials could be argued as violating this duty, particularly if the agreement intends for a more expeditious return to safeguard sensitive information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 90-day delay before returning confidential materials, which contradicts the obligation of good faith implied in contracts. The Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304 states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." While the original text implies a prompt return, aligning with good faith, the 90-day delay weakens this, potentially allowing misuse of confidential information. The delay contradicts the principle of safeguarding sensitive information, undermining the good faith expected in the contract's performance.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that the obligations pertaining to confidentiality and use of the Evaluation Material set forth in sections (2) through (6) above, and this clause (9), shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement until the expiration of the period ending five (5) years after the date first set forth above.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that the obligations pertaining to confidentiality and use of the Evaluation Material set forth in sections (2) through (6) above, and this clause (9), shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement until the expiration of the period ending one (1) year after the date first set forth above.
perturbation.explanation: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from 5 years to 1 year. For some types of confidential information, this may be insufficient and fall short of what is considered reasonable protection, particularly if trade secrets are involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may not provide adequate protection for trade secrets, potentially violating the Economic Espionage Act if the information misappropriated qualifies as a trade secret and causes economic harm. The reasonableness of the duration is a factor in determining trade secret protection.
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from 5 years to 1 year, which may not provide adequate protection for trade secrets, as stated in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. Snippet 1, referencing the Economic Espionage Act, indicates the importance of protecting trade secrets (18 U.S.C. 1 note), implying that a shorter confidentiality period could be insufficient and therefore create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives, only if the breach was intentional and directly authorized by the party.
perturbation.explanation: The modification limits liability for breaches by representatives to only intentional and authorized breaches. This significantly reduces the scope of responsibility and could conflict with agency law principles where parties can be liable for the actions of their agents, even without direct authorization.
perturbation.law_explanation: Limiting liability to only intentional and authorized breaches by representatives contradicts the doctrine of respondeat superior, which holds employers (principals) liable for the negligent acts of their employees (agents) committed within the scope of their employment. The unauthorized action of the agent can still be under the scope of employment.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: respondeat superior | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you respondeat superior Respondeat superior is a legal doctrine, most commonly used in [wex:tort], that holds an employer or principal legally responsible for the wrongful acts of an employee or agent , if such acts occur within the scope of the employment or agency. Typically when respondeat superior is invoked, a plaintiff will look to hold both the employer and the employee liable . As such, a court will generally look to the doctrine of joint and several liability when assigning damages . Jurisdictional Differences There is not a national standard for respondeat superior . Because states create their own standards for the doctrine, different jurisdictions will use different tests to prove respondeat superior . However, most jurisdictions will use 1 of the following 2 tests: Benefits Test When the employee’s social or recreational pursuits on the employer’s premises after hours are endorsed by the express or implied permission of the employer and are conceivably of some benefit to the employer, then the employer is liable for harm resulting from the employee’s actions. Characteristics Test If the employee's action is common enough for that job that the action could be fairly deemed to be characteristic of the job, then the employer will be liable for harm resulting from the employee’s actions. Strict Liability Comparison A court will choose to apply the doctrine of respondeat superior to an employer, regardless of how closely the employer was monitoring the employee. As such, respondeat superior may be compared with strict liability . Exceptions Independent Contractors Respondeat superior applies to employees, but not to independent contractors . The Third Restatement of Torts helps to outline the difference between an employee and an independent contractor for the purpose of respondeat superior . It presents a fairly thorough balancing test : the extent of control that the agent and the principal have agreed the principal may exercise over details of the work whether the agent is engaged in a distinct occupation or business whether the type of work done by the agent is customarily done under a principal’s direction or without supervision the skill required in the agent’s occupation whether the agent or the principal supplies the tools and other instrumentalities required for the work and the place in which to perform it the length of time during which the agent is engaged by a principal whether the agent is paid by the job or by the time worked whether the agent’s work is part of the principal’s regular business whether the principal and the agent believe that they are creating an employment relationship whether the principal is or is not in business. the extent of control that the principal has exercised in practice over the details of the agent’s work." Federal Employees Under the Westfall Act , federal employees will not be held liable for wrongdoings committed during the scope of their employment. Further Reading For more on the doctrine of respondeat superior , please see this Santa Clara Law Review article , this Michigan Law Review article , and this Louisiana Law Review article . Keywords tort law vicarious liability liability Agency AGENCY LAW joint and several liability JOINT-AND-SEVERAL LIABILITY Wex accidents & injuries (tort law) standards of tort liability tort damages wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits liability for breaches by representatives to only intentional and authorized breaches. This directly contradicts the doctrine of respondeat superior, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, holds an employer or principal legally responsible for the wrongful acts of an employee or agent if such acts occur within the scope of the employment or agency. The modification attempts to narrow the scope of liability, which undermines the principle that employers can be held responsible for their employees' actions within the scope of employment, regardless of authorization. The changed text significantly weakens the established legal responsibility under respondeat superior.

perturbation.original_text: In no event shall either party be liable for consequential or punitive damages.
perturbation.changed_text: In no event shall either party be liable for any damages, including direct damages.
perturbation.explanation: The modification eliminates liability for all damages, including direct damages. This is an unusual and potentially unenforceable clause, as it effectively nullifies the agreement's purpose by removing any remedy for breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Enforceability of Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: Eliminating all damage remedies, including direct damages, renders the contract illusory and potentially unenforceable. Section 2-714 of the UCC implies there are minimum remedies for breach. The contract can be deemed unenforceable because it lacks mutuality of obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-714. Buyer's Damages for Breach in Regard to Accepted Goods. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-714. Buyer's Damages for Breach in Regard to Accepted Goods. (1) Where the buyer has accepted goods and given notification (subsection (3) of Section 2-607 ) he may recover as damages for any non-conformity of tender the loss resulting in the ordinary course of events from the seller's breach as determined in any manner which is reasonable. (2) The measure of damages for breach of warranty is the difference at the time and place of acceptance between the value of the goods accepted and the value they would have had if they had been as warranted, unless special circumstances show proximate damages of a different amount. (3)In a proper case any incidental and consequential damages under the next section may also be recovered. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes liability for *any* damages, including direct damages, which contradicts the UCC's implied minimum remedies for breach, as indicated by Section 2-714. This section allows a buyer to recover damages for non-conformity of goods, which is directly undermined by the modified contract clause eliminating all liability for damages. The original text only limited consequential or punitive damages, leaving direct damages as a recourse, which is aligned with 2-714. The changed text eliminates this recourse, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute between the parties arising out of or connected to this Agreement or its enforceability or interpretation, including any injunctive relief, shall be brought and resolved solely in the federal or state courts located within the Southern District of New York, and the parties hereby submit to the personal jurisdiction of said courts.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute between the parties arising out of or connected to this Agreement or its enforceability or interpretation, including any injunctive relief, shall be resolved through mandatory arbitration, selected by the disclosing party, with no right to appeal.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates litigation in New York courts. The modified text mandates binding arbitration with no appeal rights, selected by one party. This may conflict with state laws regarding fairness and unconscionability in arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Federal Arbitration Act generally favors arbitration, a clause granting one party complete control over the selection of the arbitrator and eliminating all appeal rights could be challenged as unconscionable and violating fundamental fairness, potentially rendering the arbitration agreement unenforceable under Section 2 of the FAA, especially if it is shown to be fundamentally unfair.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 9 - ARBITRATION 9 U.S.C. CHAPTER 1—GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 1. “Maritime transactions” and “commerce” defined; exceptio
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies dispute resolution in New York courts. The changed text mandates binding arbitration with no right to appeal, selected solely by the disclosing party. The Law Explanation suggests this could be challenged as unconscionable and violating fundamental fairness under the FAA. Scraped Law Snippet 2, U.S.C. Title 9, generally addresses arbitration, and while it doesn't explicitly prohibit the selection process or eliminate appeals, the Law Explanation interprets that such clauses could render the agreement unenforceable. This creates a contradiction as the original agreement ensures access to courts, while the changed agreement potentially bypasses this with a biased, non-appealable arbitration.
